items.title: Detections for the Axios supply chain compromise
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting Axios npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 introduced a malicious transitive dependency (plain-crypto-js@4.2.1) that executed during installation. The attack deploys cross-platform payloads across Linux, Windows, and macOS through a consistent pattern: Node.js spawns OS-native shells to retrieve and execute remote payloads in detached or hidden contexts. Linux vic
items.ai_summary: The Axios supply chain compromise is a technical attack where a malicious transitive dependency is introduced in npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4, executing during installation and deploying cross-platform payloads. This has a real-world impact of allowing attackers to gain access to systems and execute remote payloads. The most important defender action is to update Axios to a version outside of the affected range.

graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Unmasking The 64-bit Variant of the Infamous Lumma Stealer
items.description: Gen Threat Labs has identified Remus, a new 64-bit infostealer attributed to the Lumma Stealer family, emerging after Lumma's takedown and the doxxing of its alleged core members. First campaigns date back to February 2026, with the malware switching from Steam/Telegram dead drop resolvers to EtherHiding and employing new anti-analysis checks. Remus shares multiple characteristics with Lumma inclu
items.ai_summary: Remus, a 64-bit infostealer, is a new variant of the Lumma Stealer family, employing anti-analysis checks and using EtherHiding for dead drop resolvers. This malware has been active since February 2026, with campaigns leveraging T1497 and T1102 tactics. To defend against Remus, prioritize implementing robust sandbox evasion detection and web service monitoring.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1497| B[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    B -->|T1102| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Leveling Up with NightSpire Ransomware
items.description: NightSpire ransomware, first discovered in February 2025, presents a categorization challenge regarding whether it operates as Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS). Analysis of two incidents from December 2025 and March 2026 reveals significant variations in tactics, techniques, and procedures between attacks. The March 2026 incident involved threat actors installing Chrome Remoting Desktop and AnyDesk
items.ai_summary: NightSpire ransomware poses a significant threat due to its evolving tactics and techniques, including the use of Chrome Remoting Desktop and AnyDesk for remote access. Its ability to encrypt data for impact, as seen in T1486, makes it a high-priority concern. To defend against NightSpire, organizations should prioritize securing remote access tools and monitoring for suspicious activity related to data encryption.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1486| C[Impact]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Exploitation for Client Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: North Korea's Contagious Interview Campaign Spreads Across 5 Ecosystems, Delivering Staged RAT Payloads
items.description: A North Korean threat operation has published malicious packages across npm, PyPI, Go Modules, crates.io, and Packagist, impersonating legitimate developer tooling. The campaign uses GitHub aliases including golangorg and aokisasakidev to distribute staged malware loaders that contact actor-controlled infrastructure, retrieve payloads from Google Drive, and deliver platform-specific second-stage m
items.ai_summary: The North Korean threat operation compromises software supply chains by publishing malicious packages across multiple ecosystems, delivering staged RAT payloads. This campaign uses social engineering tactics to impersonate legitimate developer tooling, ultimately leading to remote access and potential data breaches. Defenders should prioritize verifying the authenticity of developer tools and monitoring for suspicious activity related to these packages.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Brief: Widespread Impact of the Axios Supply Chain Attack
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack compromised the Axios JavaScript library after threat actors hijacked an npm maintainer account, releasing malicious versions v1.14.1 and v0.30.4. These versions contained a hidden dependency called plain-crypto-js, which deployed a cross-platform remote access Trojan affecting Windows, macOS, and Linux systems. The malware performed reconnaissance, established
items.ai_summary: The Axios supply chain attack compromised the JavaScript library by hijacking an npm maintainer account, releasing malicious versions that deployed a cross-platform remote access Trojan. This Trojan performed reconnaissance and established remote access on affected systems. Defenders should immediately inspect and update Axios dependencies to prevent further compromise.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic

items.title: APT28 exploit routers to enable DNS hijacking operations
items.description: Russian cyber actors APT28 have been exploiting routers to overwrite Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)/Domain Name System (DNS) settings to redirect traffic through attacker-controlled DNS servers. Resulting malicious DNS resolutions enable adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) attacks that harvest passwords, OAuth tokens and other credentials for web and email related services. This puts organi
items.ai_summary: APT28 exploits routers to overwrite DHCP/DNS settings, enabling DNS hijacking operations and adversary-in-the-middle attacks to harvest credentials. This threat has a significant real-world impact, as it allows attackers to intercept sensitive information. To defend against this threat, organizations should prioritize monitoring and securing their network infrastructure, particularly routers and DNS servers.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1557| B[Adversary-in-the-Middle]:::tactic
    B -->|T1583.002| C[DNS Server]:::tactic
    C -->|T1584.002| D[DNS Server]:::tactic
    D -->|T1557| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Bogus website fakes virus scan, installs Venom Stealer instead
items.description: A fake website impersonating Avast antivirus is tricking users into infecting their computers with Venom Stealer malware. The site runs a fake virus scan, claims to find threats, and prompts users to download a malicious file disguised as a system cleaner. The malware, identified as part of the Venom Stealer family, steals browser credentials, session cookies, cryptocurrency wallets, and other sen
items.ai_summary: The threat is a fake Avast antivirus website that tricks users into downloading Venom Stealer malware, which steals browser credentials, session cookies, and cryptocurrency wallets. This malware is spread through user execution of a malicious file disguised as a system cleaner. Defenders should block access to known malicious websites and educate users on the dangers of fake antivirus websites.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1555.003| D[Credentials from Web Browsers]:::tactic
    D -->|T1539| E[Steal Web Session Cookie]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ringing in Chaos: How TeamPCP Weaponized the Telnyx Python SDK
items.description: TeamPCP uploaded malicious versions of the telnyx Python SDK to PyPI, compromising a package with 750,000 monthly downloads. The attack uses a three-stage architecture: a trojanized package triggers a platform-specific loader, which downloads a second-stage payload hidden in a WAV file using steganography, deploying a credential harvester. The harvester steals various credentials, encrypts them, a
items.ai_summary: TeamPCP compromised the Telnyx Python SDK on PyPI, using a three-stage architecture to deploy a credential harvester. The attack involves a trojanized package, a platform-specific loader, and steganography to hide a second-stage payload. Defenders should immediately remove the compromised package and monitor for credential theft.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608| B[Stage Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix Malware Uses macOS Script Editor to Deliver Atomic Stealer
items.description: Jamf Threat Labs discovered a ClickFix-style macOS attack that abuses the applescript:// URL scheme to launch Script Editor and deliver an Atomic Stealer infostealer payload — bypassing Terminal entirely.
items.ai_summary: ClickFix malware uses applescript:// URL scheme to launch macOS Script Editor, delivering Atomic Stealer infostealer payload without Terminal access. This bypasses traditional security controls. Defenders should monitor for suspicious applescript:// URLs and block access to Script Editor.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1649| B[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: New Lua-based malware LucidRook observed in targeted attacks against Taiwanese organizations
items.description: Cisco Talos observed a spear-phishing attack delivering LucidRook, a newly identified stager that targeted a Taiwanese NGO in October 2025. The metadata in the email suggests that it was delivered via authorized mail infrastructure, which implies potential misuse of legitimate sending capabilities.
items.ai_summary: LucidRook is a Lua-based malware used in targeted spear-phishing attacks against Taiwanese organizations. The malware is delivered via phishing emails, potentially exploiting authorized mail infrastructure. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for phishing attempts and unauthorized email activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: CVE-2026-29872: A cross-session information disclosure vulnerability exists in the awesome-llm-a...
items.description: A cross-session information disclosure vulnerability exists in the awesome-llm-apps project in commit e46690f99c3f08be80a9877fab52acacf7ab8251 (2026-01-19). The affected Streamlit-based GitHub MCP Agent stores user-supplied API tokens in process-wide environment variables using os.environ without proper session isolation. Because Streamlit serves multiple concurrent users from a single Python proc
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-29872 is a cross-session information disclosure vulnerability in the awesome-llm-apps project, allowing attackers to access user-supplied API tokens. This vulnerability affects Streamlit-based GitHub MCP Agents, which serve multiple concurrent users from a single Python process. Defenders should isolate user sessions to prevent token disclosure.

items.title: CVE-2025-66215: OpenSC is an open source smart card tools and middleware. Prior to version 0.27....
items.description: OpenSC is an open source smart card tools and middleware. Prior to version 0.27.0, an attacker with physical access to the computer at the time user or administrator uses a token can cause a stack-buffer-overflow WRITE in card-oberthur. The attack requires crafted USB device or smart card that would present the system with specially crafted responses to the APDUs. This issue has been patched in ve
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-66215 is a stack-buffer-overflow WRITE vulnerability in OpenSC prior to version 0.27.0, exploitable via a crafted USB device or smart card. An attacker with physical access can leverage this issue to gain unauthorized access. Defenders should ensure timely patching and implement strict access controls.

items.title: CVE-2025-66038: OpenSC is an open source smart card tools and middleware. Prior to version 0.27....
items.description: OpenSC is an open source smart card tools and middleware. Prior to version 0.27.0, sc_compacttlv_find_tag searches a compact-TLV buffer for a given tag. In compact-TLV, a single byte encodes the tag (high nibble) and value length (low nibble). With a 1-byte buffer {0x0A}, the encoded element claims tag=0 and length=10 but no value bytes follow. Calling sc_compacttlv_find_tag with search tag 0x00 r
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-66038 is a vulnerability in OpenSC prior to version 0.27.0, allowing an attacker to exploit a public-facing application (T1190) via a compact-TLV buffer overflow. This can lead to arbitrary code execution. Defenders should update OpenSC to version 0.27.0 or later.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2025-66037: OpenSC is an open source smart card tools and middleware. Prior to version 0.27....
items.description: OpenSC is an open source smart card tools and middleware. Prior to version 0.27.0, feeding a crafted input to the fuzz_pkcs15_reader harness causes OpenSC to perform an out-of-bounds heap read in the X.509/SPKI handling path. Specifically, sc_pkcs15_pubkey_from_spki_fields() allocates a zero-length buffer and then reads one byte past the end of that allocation. This issue has been patched in versi
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-66037 is a heap read vulnerability in OpenSC prior to version 0.27.0, exploitable via a crafted input to the fuzz_pkcs15_reader harness. This issue allows an attacker to potentially read sensitive information. Defenders should update OpenSC to version 0.27.0 or later to mitigate this vulnerability.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2025-49010: OpenSC is an open source smart card tools and middleware. Prior to version 0.27....
items.description: OpenSC is an open source smart card tools and middleware. Prior to version 0.27.0, an attacker with physical access to the computer at the time user or administrator uses a token can cause a stack-buffer-overflow write in GET RESPONSE. The attack requires crafted USB device or smart card that would present the system with specially crafted responses to the APDUs. This issue has been patched in ver
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-49010 is a stack-buffer-overflow write vulnerability in OpenSC prior to version 0.27.0, exploitable via physical access to a computer and a specially crafted USB device or smart card. This issue allows an attacker to replicate malware through removable media. To defend, ensure timely patching of OpenSC to version 0.27.0 or later.

items.description: A security vulnerability has been detected in osrg GoBGP up to 4.3.0. items.description: In KubePlus 4.1.4, the mutating webhook and kubeconfiggenerator components have an SSRF vulnerability when processing the chartURL field of ResourceComposition resources. The field is only URL-encoded without validating the target address. items.description: MRCMS V3.1.2 contains an unauthenticated directory enumeration vulnerability in the file management module. The /admin/file/list.do endpoint lacks authentication controls and proper input validation, allowing remote attackers to enumerate directory contents on the server without any credentials.

items.title: CVE-2026-27508: Smoothwall Express versions prior to 3.1 Update 13 contain a reflected cross-sit...
items.description: Smoothwall Express versions prior to 3.1 Update 13 contain a reflected cross-site scripting vulnerability in the /redirect.cgi endpoint due to improper sanitation of the url parameter. Attackers can craft malicious URLs with javascript: schemes that execute arbitrary JavaScript in victims' browsers when clicked through the unsanitized link.

items.description: Smoothwall Express versions prior to 3.1 Update 13 contain a stored cross-site scripting vulnerability in the /cgi-bin/vpnmain.cgi script due to improper sanitation of the VPN_IP parameter. items.title: The Hidden Security Risks of Shadow AI in Enterprises
items.description: As AI tools become more accessible, employees are adopting them without formal approval from IT and security teams. While these tools may boost productivity, automate tasks, or fill gaps in existing workflows, they also operate outside the visibility of security teams, bypassing controls and creating new blind spots in what is known as shadow AI. While similar to the phenomenon of

items.description: In a previous diary [1], we looked to see how numbers were used within passwords submitted to honeypots. One of the items of interest was how dates, and more specifically years, were represented within the data and how that changed over time. items.title: A Little Bit Pivoting: What Web Shells are Attackers Looking for?, (Tue, Apr 7th)
items.description: Webshells remain a popular method for attackers to maintain persistence on a compromised web server. Many "arbitrary file write" and "remote code execution" vulnerabilities are used to drop small files on systems for later execution of additional payloads. The names of these files keep changing and are often chosen to "fit in" with other files. Webshells themselves are also often used by parasitic attacks to compromise a server. Sadly (?), attackers are not always selecting good passwords either. In some cases, webshells come with pre-set backdoor credentials, which may be overlooked by a less sophisticated attacker.
items.workflow_graph:

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.

items.title: Kimwolf Botnet Swamps Anonymity Network I2P
items.description: For the past week, the massive "Internet of Things" (IoT) botnet known as Kimwolf has been disrupting the The Invisible Internet Project (I2P), a decentralized, encrypted communications network designed to anonymize and secure online communications. I2P users started reporting disruptions in the network around the same time the Kimwolf botmasters began relying on it to evade takedown attempts against the botnet's control servers.
